exemplar.title:
How has Mercedes-Benz addressed and reflected gender roles and stereotypes
using language features and visual elements in its advertising to resonate with
its audience over time?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How has Mercedes-Benz addressed and reflected gender roles and stereotypes
using language features and visual elements in its advertising to resonate with
its audience over time?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused on Mercedes-Benz gender portrayal, and consistently drives the analysis
Topic is communicated with clear purpose using defined key terms (e.g., âgender roles,â âvisual elementsâ) and logical headings
Methodology gathers primary ads across six decades and justifies their relevance, showing evidence of informed selection
weaknesses: Methodological range is moderate, relying heavily on journalistic and blog sources rather than peer-reviewed studies
Explanation of selection criteria for advertisements is not fully transparent
Occasional digressions (e.g., extended colour-psychology discussion) momentarily dilute focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the investigation demonstrates a clear and effective focusâanchored by a sharply defined research question and coherent use of terminologyâthe methodology, though generally appropriate, lacks full rigor and depth. The student shows evidence of informed source selection and consistently ties sections back to the central inquiry, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band for Focus and Method.

strengths: Good overall knowledge of Mercedes-Benzâs advertising history and basic gender theory concepts (e.g., objectification, colour symbolism)
Terminology such as âstereotype,â âtarget audience,â and âauthorial choicesâ is applied accurately in most contexts
Primary adverts are correctly integrated with captions and credits
weaknesses: Reliance on anecdotal or journalistic sources limits academic grounding
Key theoretical concepts lack rigorous citation from media or gender-studies scholarship
Occasional imprecision in terminology (e.g., misusing âphysiologicalâ instead of âpsychologicalâ colour factors)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates a coherent understanding of the topic and generally accurate use of subject-specific concepts, but the uneven academic grounding and occasional conceptual imprecision constrain it to the lower half of the 3â4 band for Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: Analysis links visual and linguistic features (colour, composition, copy lines) to gendered meanings in a chronological framework
Some evaluative commentary on the implications of shifting gender portrayals for brand identity and social attitudes
Reflective questions and incisive passages (e.g., women as accessories) show emerging critical evaluation
weaknesses: Depth of research is adequate but relies mostly on accessible online commentary rather than scholarly studies
Analysis sometimes slips into descriptive summary without theoretical backing (semiotic frameworks or media theories)
Limited exploration of counter-arguments or deeper source evaluation (credibility, bias)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation moves beyond basic description to present a coherent argument and some critical evaluation, but uneven theoretical application and limited depth place it at the lower end of the 4â6 band for Critical Thinking.

strengths: Clearly appropriate overall structure with title page, contents page, page numbers, and labeled sections
Headings effectively guide the reader through thematic shifts (e.g., âShift Towards Inclusive and Diverse Representationsâ)
Images are embedded with captions and credits, and the word count is provided
weaknesses: Formatting inconsistencies in figure captions (missing years) and bibliography style
Occasional orphaned captions and misaligned paragraph breaks disrupt flow
The conclusion is cut short mid-sentence, and some long alt-text entries detract from readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets core EE conventions with a logical, reader-friendly structure, but minor formatting inconsistencies and incomplete elements prevent a fully flawless layout, placing it in the 3â4 band for Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Coverage of sources and methods:** The student gathers a series of primary advertisements (1950sâ2020s) and supplements them with secondary web articles, colour-psychology pieces, and academic papers on gender in advertising. Captions, figure numbers, and a works-cited list demonstrate some methodological planning.

**Evidence of informed selection:** The adverts chosen span key decades and illustrate shifts in gender portrayal, showing awareness of the need for diachronic comparison. The student also justifies why each ad is relevant.

**Shortcomings:** The range is moderate rather than extensive; reliance on popular websites and blogs diminishes academic robustness, and the essay lacks a transparent explanation of criteria for choosing the specific adverts. Consequently, the methodology is mostly complete but not fully rigorous or âclearly effective,â placing it solidly in the 3-4 band.

**Formatting strengths:** The student provides page numbers, a complete table of contents, consistent heading styles, and in-text figure captions. The word count is stated, and images are generally well placed.

**Referencing and visuals:** MLA-style citations are attempted; most sources appear in the works-cited list. Images are credited, though some URLs are excessively long and formatting of the bibliography is uneven.

**Limitations:** Minor inconsistencies in font sizing, occasional orphaned captions, and variable citation format prevent a flawless presentation. Overall, layout considerations are present and mostly correct, warranting a solid score in the 3-4 band.

**Breadth and relevance:** The student analyses eight primary advertisements spanning six decades and supports observations with around fifteen secondary sources. Most materials directly address gender portrayal or Mercedes marketing, demonstrating adequate relevance to the RQ.

**Depth of research:** Despite covering multiple eras, the selection favours easily accessible online images and journalistic commentary; peer-reviewed academic studies are limited. Source evaluation (credibility, bias) is largely absent, and there is little evidence of archival or scholarly depth.

**Judgement:** The research thus moves beyond minimal but does not yet constitute âgoodâ consistency or excellence, meriting a lower-mid score within the 4-6 band.

**Strengths:** The student clearly introduces the exploration of Mercedes-Benz advertising and explicitly frames the inquiry around gender roles and stereotypes. The opening paragraphs articulate the historical scope (1960sâpresent) and the specific focus on âlanguage features and visual elements,â signalling a purposeful, audience-aware topic.

**Clarity and coherence:** Key terms such as âgender roles,â âstereotypes,â and âvisual elementsâ are defined early, and headings guide the reader through a logical progression from background to analysis. The consistent return to the central idea of how the brand âresonates with its audience over timeâ maintains focus.

**Minor limitations:** Occasional digressions (e.g., lengthy colour-psychology explanations unlinked to the core argument) momentarily blur the sharpness of focus, preventing the work from reaching the very top of the band. Overall, however, the topic is communicated accurately and effectively.

**Discussion development:** The essay advances a clear line: Mercedes shifted from male-centric, stereotypical advertising to more inclusive representations. Sections on âModern-Day Advertisementsâ and âShift Towards Inclusive and Diverse Representationsâ synthesize findings and illustrate a trajectory of change.

**Evaluation and reflection:** The student comments on the implications of these shifts for brand identity and social attitudes, indicating some evaluative thinking. However, critical engagement with limitations (e.g., potential continued tokenism, commercial motives) is brief, and opposing perspectives are not fully explored.

**Overall appraisal:** The discussion shows coherence and some critical insight but lacks the depth and nuance needed for the higher bands, fitting best at the lower end of 4-6.

**Analytical approach:** The student links specific visual and linguistic features (colour, camera angle, copy lines) to gendered meanings, showing an ability to interpret semiotic cues. Chronological comparison highlights change over time, moving analysis beyond mere description.

**Support and coherence:** Examples are usually connected to the thesis, yet some assertions (e.g., "red evokes arousal, therefore readers feel urgency to buy") are anecdotal and lack empirical backing. Theoretical models (e.g., Goffmanâs gender displays) are mentioned implicitly but not rigorously applied.

**Effectiveness:** Because the reasoning sometimes slips into narrative summary and because counter-arguments or alternative readings are minimal, the depth is uneven. Arguments are âadequatelyâ supported rather than âmostlyâ or âwellâ supported, positioning the work at the lower edge of the 4-6 band.

**Compliance with EE conventions:** The essay contains a title page, word count, contents page with page numbers, clearly labelled sections, and a works-cited list, fulfilling core structural requirements.

**Logical flow:** Headings guide the reader through background, analysis, and conclusion, and visuals are embedded with captions. Paragraphing is generally coherent, aiding readability.

**Minor issues:** Some headings (e.g., âThe End of Stereotypesâ) overlap in content, and figure integration occasionally disrupts flow. Nonetheless, the overall structure is clearly appropriate, aligning with the upper band for presentation.

**Demonstrated understanding:** The student shows sound knowledge of Mercedes-Benzâs brand history, advertising strategies, and gender theory concepts (e.g., objectification, colour symbolism). References to specific campaigns (e.g., âSheâs All of Usâ) and historical ads evidence familiarity with the material.

**Use of sources:** Secondary commentary and primary adverts are woven into the narrative, and most citations are appropriate. However, many sources are journalistic or blog-based, and theoretical frameworks from media or gender studies are only lightly referenced, limiting the sophistication of source use.

**Overall:** Knowledge is good and generally coherent, but because some claims remain anecdotal and the academic grounding is uneven, the strand fits best in the upper half of the 3-4 band.

**Terminology applied:** The essay employs subject-specific terms such as âpathos,â âallusion,â âcolour psychology,â âobjectification,â and âauthorial choicesâ with reasonable accuracy. Concepts like âstereotype,â âinclusive representation,â and âtarget audienceâ are used consistently.

**Consistency and precision:** While generally correct, occasional imprecision appears (e.g., conflating âphysiologicalâ with âpsychologicalâ; uneven differentiation between âsexismâ and âgender stereotypingâ). Scholarly terms from semiotics or discourse analysis are largely absent, and some technical advertising jargon is used only superficially.

**Evaluation:** Usage is mostly accurate but not deeply developed or consistently sophisticated, aligning with the descriptors for band 3-4 rather than the flawless consistency required for 5-6.

**Clearly stated and sufficiently narrow:** The research question is presented verbatim on the title page and is tightly framed around one brand (Mercedes-Benz) and two analytical lenses (language and visual features) across time, ensuring manageability.

**Connected to discussion:** Each main section explicitly relates back to the research question, examining how specific adverts exemplify or challenge gender constructs. The chronological structure further reinforces the focus, demonstrating how the question drives the narrative.

**Room for sharper delineation:** While focused, some sub-sections (e.g., the comparison with BMW/Audi) drift slightly beyond the central RQ, indicating partialânot flawlessâfocus. Nevertheless, the link between question and analysis remains strong and purposeful.

annotations.comment: The âThe End of Stereotypesâ header crisply frames the thematic transition toward the studyâs concluding arguments.

The research question is sharply defined, specifying Mercedes-Benz, gender roles and stereotypes, language and visual analysis across time. This precise focus strengthens the explorationâs direction and ensures all arguments remain closely tied to the central inquiry.
The clear inclusion of the word count at the beginning meets the IB Extended Essay requirement and demonstrates compliance with formatting guidelines.
The detailed description of Fig. 3 is clear but remains largely descriptive. Introduce semiotic theory (e.g., signifier/signified) to deepen interpretive analysis.
The caption lacks the sourceâs publication year. Include full citation details in each figure caption to meet academic standards.
The critique of Fig. 5âs rhetorical pathos and its reinforcement of gender stereotypes is well supported and demonstrates effective analytical depth.
Claims about competitor brands lack direct examples; include specific ads or citations from BMW, Audi and Porsche to substantiate the comparison.
The term âphysiological factorâ is misapplied when discussing colour; replace with âpsychologicalâ to maintain conceptual accuracy in colour psychology discourse.
The choice of a 1963 primary advertisement effectively illustrates male-centric imagery; the clear caption and credit support source transparency.
The stereotypes table is useful but formatting is inconsistent. Standardize heading hierarchy and cell alignment to improve readability.
Posing reflective questions about womenâs overlooked desires effectively highlights gaps in historical advertising strategies and deepens critical engagement.
The header for the modern-day section clearly signals a chronological and thematic shift, supporting coherent structure.
The claim that Mercedes is âaware of its social impactâ is made without citation. Reference corporate reports or CSR publications to substantiate this assertion.
The table of contents is comprehensive and well formatted, listing all major sections with accurate page numbers, which enhances navigation and overall structure.
Paragraph breaks are misaligned, splitting sentences mid-flow. Adjust formatting so each paragraph remains intact for clearer readability.
The clear section header for the Introduction supports logical organization and guides the reader effectively through the argumentâs development.
The detailed brand history is interesting but dominates the introduction; tighten this to foreground the research question and maintain focus on gender analysis.
Definitions of gender norms lack academic citation; integrate theoretical sources on gender studies to substantiate these claims and deepen scholarly grounding.
The discussion of early ads would benefit from applying a media theory framework (e.g., Goffmanâs gender displays) to move beyond description toward deeper critical insight.
This passage critically reflects on historical gender roles and successfully connects visual placement to societal expectations.
Phrases like âstrategically delude their audienceâ are rhetorically strong but risk overstatement. Soften claims or support with evidence to maintain scholarly tone.
Colour interpretations (e.g., black conveys prestige) are asserted without referencing established colour-psychology research; cite relevant studies to substantiate these claims.
The analysis linking womenâs portrayal as accessories to objectification is incisive and demonstrates a strong critical evaluation of visual elements.
The long alt-text replicates the entire methodology in-line. Condense alt text to key details and reserve full descriptions for captions.
The caption for Fig. 8 lacks italicization of the adâs title and quotation marks. Adhere to MLA figure caption conventions.
The historical overview of colour psychology is descriptive; integrate theoretical sources on colour semiotics to elevate analytical rigour.
The âComparison Between Brandsâ section introduces a clear methodological step for comparative analysis, reinforcing the studyâs focus and logical progression.
This final statement succinctly encapsulates the studyâs insights on progress, inclusivity and brand evolution, providing a clear evaluative finish.
The final reference entry is incomplete (âJournal ofâ truncated). Verify each citation entry to ensure no missing information.
References continue onto a second page without clear numbering restart. Maintain sequential numbering or label continuation to uphold formatting consistency.
While Fig. 6 is well chosen, its analysis remains general. Apply a specific feminist media theory lens to uncover deeper ideological underpinnings.
The discussion of inclusive shifts is coherent but stops short of critically examining commercial motives; consider evaluating potential tokenism or marketing agendas.
Fig. 7 is visually engaging, but its âfigureâ formatting diverges from earlier captions. Standardize caption style for consistency.
The conclusion paragraph is cut mid-sentence. Ensure complete, grammatically coherent sentences to provide a satisfying closure.
The Works Cited list mixes MLA, hyperlink and DOI styles inconsistently. Standardize all entries to one citation format for professional presentation.
The âShift Towards Inclusive and Diverse Representationsâ header effectively signals a major analytical pivot and maintains reader engagement.
